input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology, featuring lower and upper level chassis components and autonomy elements. Operational domain is Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems within automotive ADAS, characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) requiring computational offloading for critical functions like lead vehicle detection, speed computation, and emergency braking commands.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: 1) Data Monitoring via a user interface displaying real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, with capabilities to save data and generate reports; 2) Computation Offloading where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed computation, and actuation commands are processed externally to alleviate resource-constrained on-board ECUs; 3) Remote Management allowing operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands. Target users include operators for real-time monitoring and control, and developers for troubleshooting using collected data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as middleware, and application modules implemented in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink to support low-latency communication, data collection, and system management.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Clock synchronization accuracy requirement of 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications due to hardware limitations, aligning with industry standards mandating stricter than 100 ms synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Three distinct entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical) deployed on separate hardware components; each entity's Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as individual ROS2 packages with ROS2 nodes, where Physical Twin Entity deviates from ISO 23247 RA by hosting general automotive functionality like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, and User Interface FE is split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architectural design based on ISO 23247 RA, with software components mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages; User Entity contains 3D Visualization and Control Panel, Digital Twin Entity handles data management and control, Physical Twin Entity manages hardware resources and automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, though Physical Twin Entity's scope deviates from the standard by including general automotive functionality.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Compliance with industry clock synchronization standards requiring 100 ms accuracy for safety-critical vehicular applications, though specific data exchange protocols and external system dependencies are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system utilizes a SQL database for structured data storage and management, with data collection components handling real-time sensor inputs. Computational models incorporate DBSCAN clustering algorithms for spatial pattern recognition and Kalman filters for state estimation and trajectory tracking within the Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller for converting desired speed into motor voltage commands, with lower-level control mechanisms for chassis steering and speed regulation as specified in Section III.B and Table I.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing components include a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for high-resolution 3D point cloud acquisition and an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera for stereo vision depth measurements. Vehicle speed and steering angle data are collected through the vehicle's CAN bus interface, distinct from the LiDAR and camera sensors.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin maintains high-fidelity real-time replication through Requirement R7 (maximum network latency of 100 ms) and Requirement R8 (clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms), ensuring precise temporal alignment between physical and digital states.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor/steering actuation) is collected via Data Collecting FE and processed by Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking for raw LiDAR data. Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. ROS2 handles intra-component communication, while UDP over Wi-Fi transports data to user interfaces for real-time monitoring of ACC-relevant data including object tracks and projected driving paths.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands such as enabling/disabling ACC, setting target speed, and emergency braking are generated by the Controlling FE and User Interface FE. These commands are serialized and translated via the Data Translation component, then processed by the Actuation FE which converts desired speed into motor voltage commands for physical actuation. Remote management of ACC is enabled through the UI, with validation occurring during data translation for correct message formatting and unit consistency.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Intra-component communication uses ROS2 middleware. Inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi with unspecified transport protocol due to truncated documentation. The system must maintain network latency of at most 100 ms per requirement R7. Reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Entities are deployed across separate hardware components, with vehicle-mounted hardware handling onboard processing and external storage devices managing ego vehicle state data due to limited onboard storage capacity. Deployment is edge-based, with data storage external to the vehicle for scalability and capacity reasons. Specific cloud providers or detailed scalability mechanisms are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system supports decision-making via the User Entity providing interfaces for monitoring data and controlling Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs), while Functional Entities transform raw data into human-interpretable formats for user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247 standard, which includes four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture (Part 2), Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. The standard defines an entity-based reference architecture comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) within these entities handle specific tasks like data transformation and user interaction, with OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) cited as an example implementation for Data Collection and Device Control Entities.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with sensors and control technology. It correctly identifies the chassis layers (lower and upper) and the autonomy elements. The operational domain (ACC in ADAS) and the resource constraints of ECUs requiring offloading are well-supported by the text. Quotes: '1/10-scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower and upper level chassis, and the autonomy elements', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction comprehensively lists the three core services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with precise details matching the source. It includes the UI capabilities (real-time data, projected path, object tracks, reports), the offloading specifics (lead vehicle detection, speed computation), and remote management commands (enable/disable ACC, set speed, emergency braking). The user groups (operators and developers) are also correctly identified. Quotes: 'Data Monitoring: ... user interface ... display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system ... determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', 'Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction correctly identifies 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK,' 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2),' and languages 'Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink.' It accurately describes their roles in 'low-latency communication, data collection, and system management,' as stated in Section III.B. The extraction is specific and well-supported by the source.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic correctly identifies the 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy requirement due to hardware limitations and mentions stricter industry standards. This is directly supported by the text: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The extraction accurately describes the three distinct entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical) deployed on separate hardware components and their implementation as individual ROS2 packages. It correctly identifies the deviation of the Physical Twin Entity from ISO 23247 RA by hosting general automotive functionality like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, and the splitting of the User Interface FE into 3D Visualization and Control Panel. Supported by quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'Physical Twin Entity... can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking', and 'User Interface FE has been split into two sub-components: visualization and control panel.'

The description correctly outlines the architectural design based on ISO 23247 RA, with software components mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages. It accurately details the responsibilities of the User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin Entities and notes the deviation of the Physical Twin Entity from the standard by including general automotive functionality. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity', and 'Physical Twin Entity... reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system... and can host general automotive functionality'.

The extraction correctly references the clock synchronization standards and the 100 ms accuracy requirement found in the text. However, the definition of 'horizontal_integration' pertains to integration with external systems, data exchange protocols, and interoperability standards. While the text mentions ROS2 and Wi-Fi for communication, it does not provide detailed information on external system integration or specific interoperability standards beyond the synchronization requirement. The extraction accurately notes that these details are not present. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications.'

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the SQL database for storage and mentions DBSCAN and Kalman filters. Evidence supports: 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database' and 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.' However, it incorrectly places these models within the 'Digital Twin Entity' for all functions, whereas the text places clustering and tracking in the Physical Twin Entity. It also generalizes data collection to 'sensor inputs' rather than the specific 'object tracks and ego vehicle state' mentioned in the text.

The extraction accurately describes the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and its role in converting 'desired speed into motor voltage commands'. It correctly references lower-level control for chassis steering and speed, supported by the text: 'The lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed of the vehicle' and the 'Actuation FE' description. The citation of 'Section III.B and Table I' contains a section numbering error (it is Section IV), but the content is correct.

Correctly lists the Hokuyo LiDAR and Intel Deepsense camera from Section III.B. However, it adds unsupported claims: the text does not mention a 'CAN bus interface' for speed/steering data (it mentions 'Vehicle State' generally), nor does it describe the camera as 'stereo vision' (only 'Depth Camera'). These unsupported details rely on outside knowledge.

The extraction correctly identifies the fidelity constraints based on R7 and R8. Evidence supports: 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms' and 'R8. Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.' The explanation of their role in ensuring temporal alignment is accurate and grounded in the text.

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the data flow from the physical vehicle to the digital twin, identifying specific data types (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state), processing components (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Collecting FE), and communication protocols (ROS2, Wi-Fi). It correctly describes the Data Translation component's role. Note: The source text cuts off at 'using U', but the extraction infers UDP; otherwise, it is well-grounded. Quotes: 'Data Translation component... serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle'.

Accurately describes the control flow, including specific command types (enable/disable ACC, set speed, emergency braking) and the components involved (Controlling FE, User Interface FE, Data Translation, Actuation FE). It correctly details the conversion of desired speed to motor voltage by the Actuation FE. Quotes: 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component'.

The extraction provides a highly accurate summary of the technical connections. It correctly identifies ROS2 for intra-component communication, Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication, and the 100 ms latency requirement. It also correctly notes that the specific transport protocol is unspecified in the text due to truncation and that reliability/security details are absent. Quotes: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U'.

Provides a correct and specific description of the deployment, including the use of separate hardware components for entities, vehicle-mounted processing, and external storage for state data due to capacity limits. It correctly identifies the absence of specific cloud provider details. Quotes: 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle', 'Due to ... limited storage on the vehicle'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly states that this characteristic is not in the document. The text mentions DTs as a 'product lifecycle management tool' and discusses the 'design phase' of automotive systems, but does not describe the specific lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin itself, such as transition management or lifecycle governance approaches.

The extraction correctly states that this characteristic is not in the document. While the paper describes a case study implementation steps (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation), it does not detail the specific engineering methodologies, quality assurance practices, testing/validation approaches, or version control strategies requested in the characteristic definition.

The description accurately captures the role of the User Entity and Functional Entities in providing interfaces for monitoring and control, as well as data transformation for human interaction. However, it lacks specific details about the types of insights generated (e.g., speed, steering angle) and specific user commands (e.g., emergency braking) detailed in the case study requirements.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurate regarding the ISO 23247 structure and entities, correctly listing the four parts and the entity-based architecture (OME, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). However, it slightly misattributes the OPC UA example to the 'Data Collection and Device Control Entities', whereas the source text specifically cites it as an example for the 'Data Translation FE'. Quote: 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) ... to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'.

Therefore, the assessment 'Not in Document' is accurate.

While the document mentions the system is 'safety-critical' and outlines requirements for latency and clock synchronization to support real-time operations, it does not describe specific security measures (e.g., cybersecurity protocols, authentication, access control) or safety mechanisms (e.g., fail-safes, risk assessments) as defined in the characteristic description. Thus, the assessment 'Not in Document' is accurate.
